III. 綜合測驗 說明:以下兩篇短文,共有 10 個空格,為第 21 至 30 題,每題有四個選項,請依各篇短文文意, 選出一個最適合該空格的答案。 ▲ 下篇短文共有 5 個空格,為第 21-25 題,請依短文文意,選出一個最適合該空格的答案。 As we continue to fight against COVID-19, healthcare providers (also defined as doctors of medicine) play an important role in our lives. They 21 offer a cure for patients 21 prevent unnecessary injuries. They take care of almost everything in one’s health. Most importantly, they help reduce the impact of the disease outbreaks at every opportunity. But how much do we know about the history of medicine? Probably not much. The only thing we know is that it is the outcome of many people’s knowledge and efforts. Indeed, 22 the pioneers of medicine, societies have changed the way healthcare providers deal with disease from ancient times to the present. Al-Zahrawi, a medical pioneer in Arab, 23, brought all his knowledge together in 30 books. The topics of the books moved on from basic ideas to complex ones, 24 food and health, skin diseases, and poisons. Dr. Atala, another medical pioneer at North Carolina’s Wake Forest Institute, 25 replacement tissues and organs, from kidneys to ears, since 1999. There are many other examples. Therefore, while we are battling for our health, do not forget to pay respects to healthcare providers as well as these pioneers.

The following are some tips about self-protection for ordinary people. To stay safe, wear a mask at all times in public and wash hands often. To avoid anxiety, watch or read pandemic news to understand the current situation for around 30 minutes a day. To improve your immunity, eat healthy, sleep well, exercise more, and work on stress reduction. To get health care, keep in touch with your community, friends, and family. To keep a positive attitude, make efforts and work together with one another to beat the disease.

options: Following whatever the news website says. ▲ 閱讀下文,回答第 35-37 題 Read the following emails and answer the questions. Dear Ms. Rosa Lee, Two days ago, my friend at college sent me a message about an innovative program. He said that in this program, your school is going to offer a series of courses taught by a group of distinguished professors who have achieved international fame in their fields. After receiving the message that day, I tried to search for more information on your department’s website, but in vain. Then, I got into contact with your department by phone. The secretary gave me your email address. Since I’m really into this program, it would be a great benefit to me if you could forward information regarding the requirements. I am looking forward to receiving the detailed information. Thank you. Alan Wang Dear Mr. Wang, th st Thank you so much for your interest in the innovative program from June 30 to September 1. Currently, we are renewing our website, so information online will be temporarily unavailable. That is th why you were not able to browse our web page on May 28. In addition to the latest report card and a certificate of English proficiency, the following are requirements you need to meet to apply for this program.

15 students from each partner university can apply for the program free of charge. ▲ 閱讀下文,回答第 38-42 題 Do animals have a sixth sense? Can it be used to detect a natural disaster about to strike? For scientists, the answer is positive. In 2011, a research team used motion-triggered cameras to observe the wildlife in Yanachaga National Park, the Amazon rainforest in Peru. Just in a few days prior to a 7.0 magnitude earthquake in the Peruvian Andes, the team noted the number of wild animals had decreased and eventually disappeared. Likewise, a team of US scientists attached geolocators, lightweight tracking devices, to golden-winged warblers. They found that these warblers flew away from the Cumberland Mountains of eastern Tennessee, their habitats, to Florida or even Cuba, but several days later they flew back. According to the scientists, it was deadly storms that made these warblers fly more than 900 miles. In fact, there are numerous cases of how animals, such as dogs, sharks, and elephants, behave abnormally right before a natural disaster. Scientists are hence puzzled over animals’ unusual behavior. Dr. Rachel Grant, an animal biologist at Anglia Ruskin University, said that natural disasters like earthquakes produce a force that hits the Earth’s surface, creating an atmospheric change that causes a sense of unease. Other scientists explained that it is low-frequency sounds and vibrations that allow animals to sense upcoming danger. Basically, animals can flee away from the danger they sense to favor their survival success, and humans can interpret why animals move away from the unpleasant “stimuli.” However, humans are still unable to use animals as monitoring systems effectively to protect our lives and property in time from natural disasters.
